q: The discovery of electromagnetic induction was made in 1831 by Michael Faraday and Joseph Henry working independently at the same time.
Electromagnetic induction is the physical principle behind electric motors.
Electric current can be classified into three main types: direct current (DC), alternating current (AC), and pulsed current (PC).
Direct current (DC) is characterized as a steady unidirectional current.
In a direct current, the electrons flow steadily, but their direction changes periodically.
Appliances such as flashlights, cell phones, and laptops typically run on alternating current (AC).
Alternating current (AC) is considered to be of great industrial importance.
An alternating current (AC) reverses its direction at regular time intervals.
Current flowing in power lines and normal household electricity from a wall outlet is an example of direct current (DC).
In Japan and most of America, the household voltage typically ranges between 220 V and 240 V.
Europe and most other countries in the world, including Myanmar, use an AC voltage between 220 V and 240 V.
The frequency of AC current in Japan is solely 60 Hz.
In the US, the frequency of AC current is 50 Hz.
When a coil of wire with a few turns is rotated continuously in a magnetic field, an emf is induced in the coil.
If a galvanometer is connected across the ends of a rotating coil producing AC, it will show a constant deflection.
In an AC generator, the current produced in the coil falls to zero, reverses, rises to a maximum value, and falls to zero again in each revolution.
The induced current generated by a rotating coil in a magnetic field is generally referred to as direct current.
The induced emf in an AC generator exhibits periodic variation.
The waveform of the emf produced by an AC generator is described as a square wave.
The induced emf at a particular instant is represented by $E = E_m \sin \omega t$, where $E_m$ is the maximum value of emf.
In the equation $E = E_m \sin \omega t$, $\omega$ represents the linear velocity of the rotating coil.
The angular velocity $\omega$ is numerically equal to $2 \pi f$, where $f$ is the frequency of the emf.
A rotating coil in a magnetic field forms the basic principle of a DC generator.
The magnitude of an alternating current at any instant is given by the expression $I = I_m \sin \omega t$.
Alternating current (AC) is primarily used for specialized industrial applications and rarely for household lighting.
One significant advantage of alternating current is that its voltage is relatively difficult to change.
Energy loss during long-distance distribution is greater with alternating current at high voltage than with direct current.
Joseph Henry and Michael Faraday independently discovered the methods of producing electric current from a magnetic field in 1831.
Faraday's experiments showed that closing a switch in a primary coil connected to a battery produces a continuous, steady current in a secondary coil connected to a galvanometer.
In Faraday's experiments, when the switch in the primary loop was kept closed, the galvanometer connected to the second loop showed a steady deflection.
Momentary current flow in the second loop only occurs when the magnetic field through it is changing.
Moving a current-carrying coil abruptly towards or away from another loop will induce a momentary current in the second loop.
Moving a permanent magnet towards or away from a coil will not induce any current, as only current-carrying loops can induce magnetism.
The phenomenon where a change in the magnetic lines of force through a loop causes a momentary current to flow is called resistive induction.
The current that flows due to electromagnetic induction is termed an induced current.
The electromotive force (emf) that causes the induced current to flow is known as motional emf.
Magnetic flux density $B$ is defined as the total magnetic lines of force passing perpendicularly through an entire volume of material.
Magnetic flux density $B$ is directly proportional to magnetic field strength $H$ by the relationship $B = \mu H$.
The permeability of free space, $\mu_0$, is given as $1.257 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H m}^{-1}$.
The SI unit of magnetic flux density is weber per metre squared (Wb m$^{-2}$).
Magnetic flux $\Phi_B$ measures the number of magnetic lines of force passing parallel to a surface area.
In electromagnetism, magnetic flux through a surface is the surface integral of the tangential component of the magnetic field $B$ over that surface.
For a uniform magnetic field $B$ over a plane area $A$, the magnetic flux $\Phi_B$ is simply given by $B A \cos\theta$.
The angle $\theta$ in the magnetic flux formula $B A \cos\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ and the elemental surface area vector $d\vec{A}$.
When the magnetic field is perpendicular to the surface area, the angle $\theta$ is $90^\circ$, resulting in maximum magnetic flux.
If the magnetic field is parallel to the surface area, the magnetic flux through that area is zero.
The SI unit of magnetic flux is the tesla (T).
The weber (Wb) unit is equivalent to $1 \text{ V s}$.
Magnetic field strength $H$ is a scalar quantity, while magnetic flux density $B$ is a vector quantity.
Magnetic flux $\Phi_B$ is a vector quantity.
The SI unit of magnetic flux density, Tesla (T), is equivalent to weber per cubic metre (Wb m$^{-3}$).
Magnetic flux $\Phi_B$ measures the density of magnetic lines of force passing through a volume.
The weber unit for magnetic flux is named after the German physicist James Clerk Maxwell.
According to the text, if a wire loop is not moving, there will be an induced emf only if the magnetic field itself is changing.
Michael Faraday published his law of electromagnetic induction in 1831, based on his experiments.
Faraday's law states that the magnitude of induced emf in a wire loop is inversely proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through that loop.
For a single turn of a wire loop, the induced emf is given by $E = \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$.
For a wire loop with $N$ identical turns, the induced emf is given by $E = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$.
The minus sign in Faraday's law equation $E = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ indicates the magnitude of the induced emf.
If a wire loop is moving parallel to the magnetic field lines, an induced current will flow.
Lenz's law provides the magnitude of the induced emf, while Faraday's law gives its direction.
The Russian physicist Heinrich Friedrich Emil Lenz stated his law in 1834.
Lenz's Law states that an induced emf is always in a direction that aids the change in the original magnetic flux that causes it.
Lenz's law is an example of the principle of conservation of momentum.
Mechanical work done against the opposing force experienced by a moving magnet or rotating coil is transformed into chemical energy, according to Lenz's law.
When a magnet is moved towards a coil, the magnetic flux through the coil decreases.
To oppose an increase in magnetic flux when a magnet moves towards a coil, the induced current must produce a magnetic field in the same direction as the original flux.
When the N pole of a magnet is moved towards a coil, the end of the coil nearer the magnet must act as a south pole to oppose the motion.
If the N pole of a magnet is moved towards a coil, the induced current in the coil flows in a counter-clockwise direction when viewed from the magnet.
If a magnet is moved away from a coil, the induced current will flow in the same direction as when it is moved towards the coil.
When the N pole of a magnet is moved away from the coil, the induced current flows in the clockwise direction if viewed from the magnet.
The direction of induced current is determined by applying Faraday's law.
Lenz's law states that an induced emf is always in a direction that opposes the change in the original electric flux that causes it.
According to Lenz's Law, induced current flows in the same direction as the change in magnetic lines of force.
When a magnet is moved towards a coil, the induced current in the coil generates a magnetic field that aligns with the original magnetic flux.
If the magnet is moved away from the coil, the induced current will flow in a direction that causes the coil to repel the magnet.
An induction coil is an application of electromagnetic induction.
Heinrich Ruhmkorff demonstrated how to produce an electric spark 30 cm long using an induction coil in 1851.
The primary coil of an induction coil consists of many turns of thin insulated wire.
The secondary coil of an induction coil is wound around the primary and has few turns of thick insulated wire.
In an induction coil, when the switch S is pressed, current flows through the primary, and an induced emf develops in every turn of the secondary.
Due to the secondary coil having many turns, the induced emf produced in an induction coil is relatively low.
When current flows through the primary coil of an induction coil, the soft iron core becomes an electromagnet and attracts a soft iron armature.
In an induction coil, the platinum contacts are separated when the soft iron armature is attracted, causing current to continue flowing in the primary.
A capacitor is connected across the platinum contacts in an induction coil primarily to increase the induced emf.
Induction coils are commonly used in the ignition systems of motor cars.
A generator is an electromagnetic device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy.
AC generators are also known as alternators.
AC generators operate based on the principle of Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
In a simple AC generator, the induced current flows through the two fixed carbon brushes and then through the slip rings to the external load.
In an AC generator, the current reverses its direction every 90° of coil rotation.
Doubling the speed of a rotating coil in an AC generator will double both the frequency of the alternating current and the maximum output voltage.
If the number of turns in an AC generator's coil is doubled, the frequency of the output voltage also doubles.
Increasing the area of the coil is one way to increase the induced electromotive force of a generator.
Using weaker magnets will increase the induced electromotive force of a generator.
There is a significant structural difference between an electric generator and an electric motor.
An electric motor converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.
Transformers are primarily used to convert alternating current (AC) into direct current (DC).
The core of a transformer is typically built from a solid block of high-resistance iron to minimize eddy currents.
In a transformer, the primary coil is connected to the alternating voltage source.
The voltage in the secondary coil of a transformer always has a different frequency than the voltage in the primary coil.
Laminated iron (mumetal) cores are used in transformers to maximize power loss due to induced current.
Power loss in a transformer can occur due to the heating of the wire used in the coils.
In an ideal transformer, the power input can be assumed to be equal to the power output.
A transformer that increases the voltage will proportionally increase the current in the same coil.
In an ideal transformer, the ratio of the secondary emf to the primary emf is equal to the ratio of the number of turns in the secondary to the number of turns in the primary.
A step-up transformer is characterized by having fewer turns in the secondary coil than in the primary coil.
A step-down transformer produces a voltage in the secondary coil that is smaller than the voltage applied to the primary coil.
Eddy currents are induced only in the secondary coil of a transformer, not in the iron core.
The efficiency of a transformer is the ratio of input power to output power.
Most transformers achieve a full load efficiency of 100%.
Transformers are designed to work efficiently with direct current (DC) supplies.
Connecting a transformer to a DC supply can lead to damage due to high current flowing through the input coil, causing overheating.
The primary method for transmitting electricity from power stations to consumers is through wireless transmission.
Power loss is a minor issue in the long-distance transmission of electricity.
Using very thick cables of low resistance is an economical and lightweight solution to reduce power loss during transmission.
A more practical way to reduce power loss during electricity transmission is to increase the magnitude of the transmission current.
When electrical power is transmitted at a higher voltage, the power loss due to current is reduced.
Increasing the transmission voltage from 10 kV to 20 kV at the power station reduces the power loss by a factor of two.
The national grid system is used to distribute direct current (DC) electricity across a country.
An advantage of the grid system is that power stations in areas with low demand can supply areas with high demand.
Electricity generated at power stations is initially produced at low voltage (e.g., 10 kV) for efficient long-distance transmission.
For transmission over long distances, the voltage of the alternating current is typically stepped up to very high levels, such as 230 kV.
Substations in the national grid system are responsible for stepping up the voltage before electricity reaches factories and homes.
Transformers are essential for the process of stepping up and stepping down voltages in power distribution through a grid system.
While using very thick cables of low resistance can reduce power loss, it is often an uneconomical and heavy solution.
The national grid system is a localized distribution network for electricity within a single city.
The final voltage delivered to homes and factories from the national grid system is typically 230 kV.
Electricity supplied to houses is typically in the form of direct current (DC).
A thick cable carrying electricity into a house usually contains three wires: live (L), neutral (N), and earth (E).
The live wire (L) carries a high voltage and is considered dangerous.
The neutral wire (N) in house wiring is typically at a very high voltage.
In house wiring, current enters the house through the neutral wire and returns to the substation through the live wire.
The electricity in a house is fed sequentially through the main fuse, meter, main switch, and then to the distribution box.
From the distribution box, house wiring typically branches into four main circuits: lighting, power, heating, and communication.
In the new colour code for wiring, the live wire (L) is brown, the neutral wire (N) is blue, and the earth wire (E) is striped green-yellow.
The old colour code for the live wire was black, and for the neutral wire was red.
In a lighting circuit, light bulbs are typically arranged in series across the mains line.
When bulbs are connected in parallel in a lighting circuit, switching one bulb ON or OFF affects the operation of the other bulbs.
A typical lighting circuit differs from power and heating circuits by having only two wires: live and neutral.
A lighting circuit must be connected sequentially as neutral wire, fuse, switch, load, and then to live wire.
In a power circuit (ring main circuit), distribution points are connected to the main supply in a discontinuous open circuit.
Power sockets in a house are commonly connected by means of a ring main circuit.
The ring main circuit uses only live and neutral wires, excluding an earth wire.
Since current can flow to any particular socket by two ways in a ring main circuit, thicker wires are necessary for safety.
Each main ring circuit typically uses a 30 A fuse.
Three-pin sockets and three-pin plugs are used in the ring main circuit.
Heating circuits are designed for appliances that consume relatively small amounts of power, such as radios and table lamps.
Each fuse used in a heating circuit is typically rated at 13 A.
To prevent overloading, the fuse of each circuit must be connected in the neutral (N) lead.
A circuit breaker is a manual switch that must be replaced after tripping due to excessive current.
Earthing of an electrical appliance is considered the most important safety precaution.
In any electrical appliance, the earth wire is always connected to the plastic casing for safety.
If the metal case of an un-earthed appliance becomes live, a person touching it would experience current flowing directly into the earth through the appliance's external components.
With proper earthing, current from a live metal case would pass directly into the earth through the earth wire, which has much higher resistance than a human body.
Electricity is generally harmless if used properly.
Electrical faults in appliances or circuits can lead to fires, electric shocks, and electrocution.
Only high voltage, not high current, is dangerous to humans.
Damaged insulation is one of the three possible causes of electricity-related dangers.
All electrical appliances require a single live wire to form a complete circuit.
The neutral wire is considered dangerous because it carries a high voltage.
Electrical wires are typically insulated with materials like vulcanised rubber, which are known to improve with age and use.
Bending and twisting electrical cables, such as those for hair dryers, can cause the insulation to crack and expose conducting wires.
If the vulcanised rubber covering the neutral wire is damaged and exposed, it can cause a severe electric shock if touched.
Overheating of cables primarily occurs due to very low current flow in the conducting wires.
A short circuit can result when the live wire makes electrical contact with the earth wire due to damaged insulation.
A large current from a short circuit generates a small amount of heat, which is insufficient to melt insulation or start a fire.
Overuse of extension cords and multiple plug adapters on the same circuit are typical causes of an electrical overload.
An overload happens when equipment draws less current than the supply, leading to cable cooling.
Electrical accidents are more likely to occur in dry environments.
If water from a wet floor touches an exposed live wire, it provides a conducting path that bypasses the human body.
The human body can typically withstand up to 500 mA of current without any adverse effects.
A wet human body has a much higher electrical resistance than a dry body, reducing the danger of electrocution.
Using hair dryers or electric irons with wet hands poses no significant hazard if the appliance is properly earthed.
A current of 1 mA passing through the body is at the threshold of feeling pain.
A current of 5 mA through the human body typically causes uncontrolled muscular contractions.
Pain and exhaustion, affecting breathing, are typically experienced at an electric current level of 50 mA.
Uncoordinated contraction of the heart, potentially leading to death, can occur with electric currents between 100-300 mA.
It is safe to operate electrical appliances with wet hands as long as the appliance itself is dry.
Switches, plugs, sockets, and connecting wires should always be kept in a dry condition.
